key_insight: Under nanoscratch in 316H stainless steel, martensite forms sequentially through a pressure- and shear-dependent pathway, with b3-austenite transforming to b5 and then to b1 depending on local constraint and sliding conditions.
engineering_relevance: Understanding how contact pressure and shear select different martensitic pathways can help predict tribolayer formation, galling resistance, and surface durability in stainless steels and related hardfacing alloys.
method: In-situ synchrotron X-ray nanodiffractometry during nanoscratch testing
Finite element modeling to map local strain and stress fields
Spatially resolved phase identification of b3, b5, and b1 martensite
evidence_signal: The abstract reports spatially resolved phase maps and strain-field interpretation from combined nanoscratch experiments and synchrotron measurements, rather than a conventional bulk test dataset.
watch_item: The conclusions are drawn from highly localized single-asperity scratching, so translating the pathway selection directly to real multi-asperity tribological contacts may require additional validation.
paper_title: Spatially resolved in-situ characterisation of competing martensitic transformation pathways during nanoscratch in 316H Stainless Steel
